State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 907 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

June 6, 1977

A 77-449

Robert W. Naylor
Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro
P.O. Box 7880
San Francisco, CA 94120

Re: Opinion Request No. 75-298

Dear Mr. Naylor:

This letter confirms our telephone conversation of
June 2, 1977, in which I stated that your letter of May 10,
1977, and the attached Opinion No. 75-298 in which to
Del Monte's reporting obligations. To recapitulate, Del Monte
will incur reporting obligations with respect to an agricultural
advisory board if the company's lobbyist or another employee
who is neither a board member nor a lobbyist attempts to in-
fluence the board. In the latter situation, a reporting obli-
gation will arise only if the employee spends ten percent or
more of the expended time during any one month in influenc-
ing activities and it is done in more than a de minimis of-
fort to influence the advisory board in question. Moreover,
communications between the employee and an agricultural board
member who also is a Del Monte employee are not counted in de-
termining whether the ten percent or the de minimis threshold
is met. Finally, you have asked us to clarify our time spent
on official board business by a Del Monte employee who is a
member of the board does not count toward the ten percent
threshold of important activities.

If you have any other questions, please do not
hesitate to call me.

Sincerely,

Kenneth H. Laney
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

KBP:jc

AN OFFICER OF

PILLSBURY, MADISON & SUTRO
STANDARD OIL BUILDING
225 BUSH STREET
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA
MAIL ADDRESS: P.O. BOX 7850, SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94120

TELEPHONE (415) 391-1010

WRITERS DIRECT DIAL NUMBER

983-1404

May 10, 1977

Opinion No. 75-208 (Robert C.
Herr, Attorney for Del Monte
Corporation)

Kenneth Pinney, Esq.
Staff Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Finney:

Because of the issues raised in Commissioner
Lowenstein's dissent in the subject Opinion, we are seeking
written confirmation of our understanding of Del Monte
Corporation's disclosure obligations under that Opinion with
respect to "exchanges" with specified persons and entities
related to agricultural advisory boards and other similar
state agencies (hereinafter "advisory boards").

We understand that Del Monte is required to report
as "exchanges" both the salary of any employee who serves on
an advisory board and business transactions with business
entities which are represented on an advisory board by
proprietors, partners, directors, officers, managers or 50-
plus percent owners only if Del Monte has attempted to influence
the advisory board either through its lobbyist or through
one of its employees or agents (2 Cal.Admin. Code, § 1600(a)
and (c)). We are specifically concerned about whether Del
Monte will be deemed to have influenced or attempted to
influence a board through the direct communications of any
of its employees other than Del Monte's lobbyist, and we ask
your confirmation of the following:

1. As our understanding from your letter to
me of April 1, 1977, and subsequent telephonic conversations
that, for purposes of triggering the "exchange" reporting
requirement, Del Monte will only be considered to have
influenced in advisory board through an employee who communicates with the board if Del Monte has a disclosure obligation

CA-94143
CABLE ADDRESS: EVANS
TELECOPIER TEL. (415) 398-2596

[illegible]

[illegible]

with respect to the activities of that employee--that is, if
the employee engages in reportable efforts to influence
legislative or administrative action for ten per cent or
more of his compensated time in any one month--and then only
if the mere more than a de minimis effort to influence a
particular board.

Opinion 75-208 established the further prin-
ciple that neither the time spent on official board business
by an employee who serves on an advisory board nor the time
spent by other employees communicating with any Del Monte
employee who serves on an advisory board counts toward the
ten per cent threshold of reportability (see footnote 14 on
p. 13 of the Opinion and Part 5 on pp. 13-14). Combining
these guidelines with the rule stated in paragraph 1 above,
we understand that the exchange reporting requirements with
respect to such boards will not be triggered for Del Monte
by communications of employees in their official capacities
as advisory board members and communications by other employees
with Del Monte's employees who are advisory board members.
Since no such activities would be counted in determining
whether an employee reached the ten per cent threshold or
would be reportable by Del Monte even if the employee other-
wise surpassed the ten per cent threshold.

In sum, we seek confirmation of the general prin-
ciple that Del Monte will be considered to be "influencing"
an advisory board for purposes of triggering the exchange
reporting requirements only if it is required to report
"payments to influence" the administrative action of such
advisory board under the guidelines established in Opinion
75-208.

This is a matter of continuing urgent concern for
Del Monte, and we would accordingly appreciate your early
reply.

Thank you for your assistance.

Very truly yours,

Robert W. Naylor
for
Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro

2.

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
PO BOX 867 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

April 21, 1977


Robert W. Naylor
Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro
P.O. Box 7880
San Francisco, CA 94120

Dear Mr. Naylor:

John Greenwood has asked me to respond to your
letter of March 16, 1977, concerning reporting pursuant to 2
Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18620 and 18621. You initially
posed two questions pertaining to employees who spend less
than ten percent of their time in any one month influencing
legislative or administrative action. In particular, you
have asked:

Must the employer report the subject matter and
agency which any such employee is attempting to
influence?

Must the filer report expenses incurred as the
result of activities of employees who are under
the 10 percent threshold?

In response to your first question, it is the
staff's view that the employer is not required to disclose
information concerning the subject matter and agency influenced
by an employee who spends less than ten percent of his time
in any one month attempting to influence legislative or
administrative action. With respect to your second question,
additional expenses which constitute payments to influence
legislative or administrative action within the meaning of
Gov. Code Section 82045 should be reported, even if the
employee's salary is not reportable. However, regular ongoing
overhead expenses which would be incurred in reasonably
similar amounts regardless of the employee's influencing
activities need not be reported.

In 2 Cal. Adm. Section 18620(a)(2)(D), the Commission
excluded from reporting by a lobbyist employer regular,ongoing
overhead expenses which will continue to be incurred in
reasonably similar amounts regardless of a lobbyist's activities.
Although not explicitly stated, it is the staff's view that
the same exemption applies to ongoing overhead expenses
incurred in support of other employees. The difficulty of
calculating and apportioning such costs is the same whether
the individual engaging in influencing activities is a lobbyist

[illegible] ASSISTANT ADMINISTRATOR [illegible] [illegible] [illegible] [illegible]

Robert W. Naylor
April 21, 1977
Page Two


or another employee and this difficulty outweighs any benefit
served by disclosure. On the other hand, additional costs
incurred because an employee has influenced or attempted to
influence legislative or administrative action are not
difficult to calculate. In addition, these costs clearly
would seem to be reportable pursuant to Cal. Adm. Code
Sections 1620(a)(6) and 1102l(a)(5). Accordingly, additional
expenses should be reported, even when the additional expenses
support the activities of an employee whose salary is not
includable as a payment to influence.

In response to your supplemental third question,
the staff agrees with your conclusion that an employee's
salary must be allocated and reported in all of the employee's
reportable activities, when aggregated, amount to ten percent
or more of the employee's compensated time. The regulations
do not establish a rule whereby each category of reportable
activity must amount to ten percent of the employee's compensated
time before a reporting obligation is incurred.

In subsequent telephone conversations, you also
asked what agencies must be listed in order to comply with
Section 86109(d) when an employee spends ten percent or more
of his time in any one month influencing legislative or
administrative action. Section 86109(q) requires filers to
provide "a specific description of legislative or adminis-
trative action which the person making the report has attempted
to influence." Admittedly, it is difficult to formulate a
precise actuated regarding when information should be disclosed
pursuant to this subsection. However, the staff thinks that
in general a description of legislative or administrative
action intended should be provided whenever the employee
engages in more than a de minimis effort with respect to the
legislative or administrative action in question. For example,
although counsel is of the opinion that a telephone call to an
agency or prepared and mailed a written communication is
part of an attempt to influence an agency, on the other
hand, a staff does not believe pursuant to Section 86109(q),
based on isolated and incidental contacts with an agency,
such as a single, abbreviated telephone conversation with an
agency official. If you have specific questions that repre-
sent situations beyond the extremes, I suggest that you
contact the staff for guidance.

I hope this advice will be of assistance.

Sincerely,

Natalie C. West
Staff Counsel

By: Kenneth Finney

NEW:plh

LAW OFFICES OF

PILLSBURY, MADISON & SUTRO

TELEPHONE (415) 983-1000

STANDARD OIL BUILDING

226 BUSH STREET

WRITER'S DIRECT DIAL NUMBER:

SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA

983-1404

MAIL ADDRESS: P.O. BOX 7860, SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94120

TELEX 34343
CABLE ADDRESS "Evans"
TELECOPIER: TEL.(415) 398-2540

March 16, 1977

Mr. John C. Greenwood
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Greenwood:

     This letter is in response to your request in our
telephone conversation of March 11, 1977, for a written
statement of our questions concerning the reporting obliga-
tions of lobbyist employers and §6108(b) filers under certain
circumstances.

     Under section 1850(a)(4)(C), (4) and (5) and
section 1952(a)(1), (2) and (4) of Title 2 of the California
Administrative Code, the gross compensation paid to an
employee engaging in reportable activities need not be
allocated or reported by the filer if the employee in any
one month spends less than 10 per cent of compensated time
engaging in such activities.

     As I understood the purpose and intent of the
10-per cent threshold, it was to reduce the administrative
burden for filers in keeping track of employee activities
where those activities, although involving the influencing
of legislative or administrative action, amounted to only a
negligible portion of any employed total compensated time.
Under the regulations, an employer would have to keep time
sheets and make salary allocations only for those employees
whose responsibilities were devoted in some major way to
lobbying. The adoption of the 10-per cent threshold, in my
opinion, was one of the most significant steps taken by the
Commission in making the reporting obligations of section 8610b
filers administratively manageable.

     The two questions I raised in our conversation
pertained to the employer's obligation to report nonsalary
items which arise from the activities of employees who are
under the 10-per cent threshold in a given month. Specifically:

1. Must the employer report the subject matter and agency which any such employee is attempting to influence? I recall last year receiving advice by telephone from Natalie West to the effect that such information need not be reported when the employee spends less than 10-per cent of his time on reportable activities. I would urge the Commission formally to adopt this position. It is of little or no avail to allow the exclusion of an employee's salary from the report but to require the inclusion of the subject matter of his contacts. An employer would have no choice but to require the employee to maintain a log of his contacts with state government, even if only a single telephone call a month, since any such contact could require an additional entry on the employer's report.

2. Must the filer report expenses incurred as the result of activities of employees who are under the 10 per cent threshold? Examples of such expenses would be travel expenses incurred by an employee who traveled once a year to Sacramento to testify before an agency or legislative body and duplicating expenses for the copies of his testimony furnished to officials. Again, little administrative burden is saved by excluding the allocable salary for such testimony from the report of the employer but including other incidental expenses incurred as a result of such testimony.

In reviewing sections 18620 and 18621 of the regulations, I have recalled a third question which you may wish to address in the course of any revisions: May an employee spend up to 10 per cent of his time on each category of reportable activity before his salary must be allocated or reported by his employer, or must his salary be allocated or reported when all of his reportable activities in the aggregate amount to 10 per cent or more of his compensated time? The present regulation read literally would not seem to require salary to be allocated or reported so long as no single category of reportable activity (lobbying support, communicating, urging others to communicate) exceeded 10 per cent of an employee's compensated time. On the assumption

that this was not the intent, we have been advising that
reportable activities be aggregated for purposes of deter-
mining whether employees exceed the 10-per cent threshold.

The answers to the foregoing questions will assist
us in advising a number of clients, as well as in preparing
our own Form 650, in which we regularly confront these
issues. If you require any further information in order to
answer these questions, please let me know.

Very truly yours,

Robert W. Naylor
For
Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro

Box 7680 San Francisco, CA 94120 Re: Opinion Request No. 75-208 Dear Mr. Naylor: This letter confirms our telephone conversation of June 2, 1977, in which I stated that your letter of May 10, 1977, correctly interprets Opinion No. 75-208 as it applies to Del Monte's reporting obligations. To recapitulate, Del Monte will incur reporting obligations with respect to an agricultural advisory board if the company's lobbyist or another employee who is neither a board member nor a lobbyist attempts to influence the board. In the latter situation, a reporting obligation will arise only if the employee spends ten percent or more of his compensated time during any one month in influencing activities and if he engages in more than a de minimis effort to influence the advisory board in question. Moreover, communications between the employee and an agricultural board member who also is a Del Monte employee are not counted in determining whether the ten percent or the de minimis threshold is met. Finally, you have correctly observed that time spent on official board business by a Del Monte employee who is a member of the board does not count toward the ten percent threshold of reportability. If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to call me. Sincerely, [handwritten signature] Kenneth H. Finney Staff Counsel Legal Division May 10, 1977 Opinion No. 75-208 (Robert C. Herr, Attorney for Del Monte Corporation) Kenneth Finney, Esq.
